displayTitle: 2026-06-16 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - vimeo_1201644359
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee

chunks.text: We have one hour with you, so if you would like to start with an opening statement, then great. About three minutes. Thank you. Well, thank you, and good afternoon. Today I have with me Nick Blakely, the CEO of the Ministry of Business Innovation and Employment, the Electricity Authority CEO, Sarah Gillies, and ED, Marcos Polania, who is here with officials, for the estimates. And I want to thank our members for their time.

As members know, energy powers the New Zealand economy. It keeps our homes warm and our industries running, producing jobs and opportunities across our economy. New Zealand should be proud of the fact that an increasing amount of our electricity is being produced by renewable sources. However, our energy system also has significant challenges which this government is focused on solving. The government's focus is to ensure New Zealand has abundant, affordable, reliable, and increasingly renewable energy.

To achieve these objectives, the government has made a number of key decisions to provide certainty to the market. We stopped the previous government's Lake Onslow project, allowing the sector to move forward with its own investment plans. We've put in place fast-track legislation to make consenting faster and more cost-effective. We removed the 100% renewable target by 2030, which was disincentivizing investment in thermal backup energy for when the lakes are low.

And due to these policies, we're seeing real results. Since coming into office, nearly one gigawatt of new renewable generation has been brought online. More new generation has come online in the past three years than in the previous eight years combined. Future wholesale prices have fallen noticeably since we announced our decision to procure an LNG importation terminal. And in the last quarter of last year, a record 96% of the electricity generated in New Zealand came from renewable sources.

However, there is more work to do, particularly with the significant reduction in gas supply we have seen in recent years following the previous government's decision to ban oil and gas exploration. Ultimately, we need to ensure that when the lakes are low, the wind isn't blowing, and the sun isn't shining, that we have a solution to provide the backup energy needed in a dry year. The energy gap that has been identified by MBIE in a dry year is approximately three terawatt hours of electricity.

Thanks to the Huntly firming options, half of this is covered by the coal stock-pilot Huntly. However, we need another 1.5 terawatt hours to provide that backup. After significant analysis and examining a range of options, MBIE has advised that the most cost-effective and fastest option is to procure an LNG importation terminal. The government is acting on this advice.

With a dry year expected every four years and the last one hitting in 2024, we don't have the luxury of time to spend another 10 years building a lake at the bottom of the South Island to solve this problem. We must act now, and we must act decisively. That is precisely why we are procuring an LNG importation terminal alongside putting in place an obligation on the major generator retailers that they must manage the dry year risk, with significant fines if they don't.

Alongside this decision, the government is investing in practical support to secure New Zealand's energy needs. Budget '26 invest in supporting industrial gas users to convert from gas to other forms of energy with the loan guarantee scheme, freeing up gas for those who need it the most. Supporting investment and putting solar panels in schools to reduce schools' electricity costs and increase resilience. Providing for resilience from the Middle East conflict with investment in strategic diesel reserve, which has been established in record time with diesel flowing to

those tanks this week. In addition, the government is making it easier to install rooftop solar, modernizing energy efficiency rules, and enabling technologies like EV charging, giving consumers more control over their power bills, strengthening competition, and ensuring strong, effective regulation. There is a lot going on, and we need to stay the course to make electricity more abundant, more affordable, and reliable to underpin economic growth and help New Zealanders get ahead. And with that, I'm happy to take questions.

Thank you, Minister. And we start with Honorable Megan Woods. Thank you. Thank you, Minister. Minister, you're familiar, I'm sure, with Concept Consulting's market modeling that concludes that New Zealand faces no imminent physical gas deliverability crisis and there's no urgent physical need for an LNG terminal by 2028 to meet that non-petrochemical demand. Have you received this advice?

I've seen advice from consulting that was provided to the Ministry of Business Innovation and Employment. Ultimately, the Ministry of Business Innovation and Employment has taken into account a range of pieces of advice in order to look at the dry year risk and what's required to fill that gap.

And the reality is the most recent data shows that the gas reserves reduced by another 23% in the last year. That advice was released only, I think, from MBIE last month. And that indicates another significant downgrade in our gas availability as a country. And due to that, the risk only increases. The other point that I would make is that we have a choice around-- or we have a real issue here around with the Maui field expected to close shortly, and with that, potentially,

Methanex. And through that, we lose the ability to have that flexibility which the New Zealand energy market has relied on for many, many years, which is when we have a dry year, we have the ability to have a large gas user to be able to effectively turn off their production and for that gas to be able to shift across to electricity use.

So- Without a major user like that The system becomes incredibly inflexible, and the alternative is effectively many, many industrial businesses having to turn off during a dry year. And that is economic destruction. It's called demand destruction. That's jobs, that's businesses being shut down, jobs being destroyed.

And that's not a future I think that anyone around this table wants for the New Zealand economy. And so the reality is that we have to make decisions. We've been advised of the 1.5 terawatt hour gap that needs to be filled. How we've been advised that the most cost effective and fastest solution to fill that is to have an LNG importation terminal. We are pursuing that in order to make sure that we protect our economy, put downward pressure on power bills, and make sure we fill the gap left by the dry year risk.

So supplementary to that, and the concept modeling that I've seen takes into account the latest gas figures, I believe. So have been updated to take those into account. But their modeling, and I'm sure you will have seen it too, Minister, estimates that forcing the LNG terminal into the system adds around $6.2 billion in present value costs to the New Zealand economy, and that's an undiscounted sum of around $24 billion, while largely benefiting gas pipeline and field owners. So what modeling has the minister seen that has

convinced him to go ahead with this when he has- Well-... commissioned modeling by his own officials that put, puts a cost to the New Zealand economy of $6 billion? Well, firstly, MB took-- looked at the dry year risk and went through a range of options from the cabinet paper. It's all been released before. You've seen it, I'm sure.

We referred to you that information in written questions as well, so, and in the house. So it's all available online for you to read about all of the options that were considered around how we can address this risk. And one of the points which I think the member is ignoring is the fact that this is not something that can be solved in 2037. We have a dry year risk every four years on average.

We don't have the luxury to wait till 2037 to build a $16 billion lake at the bottom of the South Island, which was what that member proposed to do when she was last in government. We don't have that luxury. So supplementary- We have a, we have a, we have a risk in front of us that needs to be addressed, it needs-- requires decisions to be made, uh, and MB's analysis is that the fastest and cheapest option before us is to have an LNG importation terminal. And something which ultimately will provide the energy that's needed into, into our system.

So we are solving a problem which the previous member ignored, uh, and we're providing it, and, uh, and, and ultimately we're al- we have already seen a significant reduction in wholesale prices on the forward market, uh, following the decision that we made as a government. We'll come to your conflation of correlation with causation in a moment, but supplementary question. So, um, the minister's saying he doesn't agree with the concept modeling, um, and that his official- Well, I've received advice from MB- No, sorry, Minister, I'll finish my question.

Hold on, hold on, hold on. Yeah, okay. Finish your question. I'll finish my question. But you're making an assertion. Um, well, I'm paraphrasing from you, you Minister. You're saying you d- that you don't agree and your officials didn't agree with the concept, uh, modeling, which for the record- I provided the advice... you did not provide to us in written questions. You redacted it because you didn't want it out there. Uh, no, sorry. Sorry, you've got- Sorry. I will hold on, member.

There are certain- Hold on. No, I listen-... all sorts of conjecture into a conversation. I think you have the opportunity to ask a supplementary question. Ask a question and get an answer. Will he please release the advice, the modeling from MB that, that, um, that, um, negates what concept he has modeled in terms of the six, the $6 billion cost to the New Zealand economy, and why he thinks it's a good idea to push ahead with something that is so clearly going to cost the country jobs and higher energy costs, because

that's what his own official's commissioned modeling shows. Uh, well, the member is wrong. The member- Well-... is completely wrong because we, uh- I'm not... firs- firstly, uh, what we are doing is protecting jobs by ensuring we have the energy needed in our economy. Um, the member's, the member's option that she is, is saying is, "Don't do anything. Ignore the dry year risk." Uh, and I'm sorry, but I-- we saw what happened following her time as Minister of Energy. It hit in 2024.

Me- the, the, the spot price hit $800 a megawatt hour, uh, in, in 2024. We suffered huge economic devastation following eight, six years while she was the minister and while she failed to actually address the risk which she knew about when she was in charge.

So- The cost to our economy, about $5.2 billion last year. Jobs, businesses were shut. The Ty Smelter went, uh, obviously it was good that they had the demand response, but ultimately that's less production and that's less GDP for our economy. That is the direct result of ignoring the problem, something this government will not do.

So supplementary. Supplementary. In the information you did release with your Cabinet paper, that you go through the scenarios that, that your officials did look at, one of which was, um, the Rankin option. Um, it was shown to, to have, um, a cost between $150 to $170 a megawatt hour for coal and 180 to 215 a megawatt hour for biomass scored the highest on there.

What I'm interested in is why locking in new significant generation capacity in that option was ruled as a negative in your assessment in the Cabinet paper. Well, there's two points I'd make. Firstly, the time it would take to build a new Rankin unit would take, uh, significantly longer than to put in place an LNG importation terminal Which was considered important. And timeliness is important here because on average, we expect to have, on average, a dry year every four years.

The second part- Sorry, just to clarify, minister, I'm not asking about timing, I'm asking about the The second-- I said two points, so I'm happy to keep answering. The minister finished his answer. Yeah. Well, the bit on the generation, not the timing. Yeah. But they're both important points to- It's a different part of the matrix... yeah, they're both important points to your question. Members, can I just say this, that for Hansard, I want to make sure it's only one person speaking at a time. Please don't talk over another person.

So minister is answering. Please, Minister, can you carry on from there? Yeah. So the first point is obviously the time it takes to build. The second is around the long-term duration of the assets is obviously a factor in terms of the time when... If you look at the modeling, the modeling suggests that, as we have greater build of renewables, which is enabled by LNG, ultimately the need for that thermal backup does reduce over time.

A new Rankine is a 50-year asset, whereas an LNG importation terminal doesn't need to be. And so there's a difference in the timing and what's required by the asset. So that's the point in terms of those factors that have been considered by MB and the options analysis. But I bring it back to the first point I was making, which is actually critically important, which is on average, we have a dry year every four years. The last one was in 2024. The next one could be, on average, would be expected around 2028.

We actually have to make decisions which address this issue quickly and in a timely manner, otherwise, we risk economic carnage on our economy, something the previous minister was prepared to have, something I am not. All right. Supplementary Scott Willis. Thank you, Madam Chair. We certainly don't want to see any more economic carnage that's been happening under this government, but what I'm really interested in is the, um, the minister having removed the levy off all electricity users to pay for LNG, how will the minister pay for LNG? Is the minister going

to compel the gen tailers to purchase LNG? Is this the way in which the import terminal will be paid for? Well, firstly, as I said in my speech last week to the Auckland Business Chamber, we won't be putting a levy on people's power bills, because that's not the right way to pay for this.

The dry year risk sits with the electricity generators and retailers. And that's the point of, in addition to what we're doing here, of putting in place the dry year regulatory framework to actually make it very clear where the risk lies, and to put obligations in place clearly on those electricity generators and retailers, to manage that risk. That is their job. That's what they should be doing.

Secondly, we have said very clearly that we will require them to pay for it. We have MB and NIFCO currently in negotiations with them around a funding model to enable them to do that, and we've said we'll have a regulatory backstop in place if they fail to come to an agreement.

So is the minister saying whether or not the gen tailers see LNG as necessary, they will pay for it? Well, we've been through a period of time where we've assessed the options, and we've come to the conclusion this is the most cost effective and fastest way to deal with this issue.

The gen tailers have not resolved the dry year regulatory risk over a very long period of time that they've had available to them to do so. And so we have put an option on the table, we are requiring them to pay for it, and we will be putting in place a regulatory obligation, which makes it incredibly clear that it is their job to fix this issue.

And if they don't, there's going to be hefty fines. Supplementary. Yep. And thank you, Minister. I appreciate the minister's clear indication of heavy intervention in the electricity sector. And given the government's willingness to intervene, why would the government not intervene in building the geothermal we know we can build, in supporting the biomass options that we know are there, in supporting the solarization, massive solarization that are already there?

If the government's willing to intervene, why has the government taken off the table all of the other options and focused on fossil fuels? Well, we want to have an option which ultimately delivers in a timely manner to address the dry year risk, and that was a key criteria in the options analysis.

Now, if it was to build a significant, to have solar. Well, solar does not provide electricity with batteries, which can currently get you through a three-month dry year winter. That option's not available to us at this stage. Battery technology is good at intraday peaks.

Soon it'll be good at day to day and maybe even longer, but that's not an option which is currently available to us today. We have the gas-fired generation plant sitting idle whilst we do not have the gas available to necessarily make sure we can-- that's exactly what happened in 2024.

We were running the coal Rankines, but we didn't have the gas to be able to fit the gap. And so we're looking at the reality of our energy system today, the kit that's available, and also looking at how can we solve this in a timely manner. And we have to make decisions, and we have to make those in a way that is in the best interest of the New Zealand economy. That is exactly what we've done.

A supplementary one. Supplementary, yes. Supplementary, yep. So there's the one-off $4 million adjustment to utilize underspends in the Getty Fund.... is put forward for offshore renewable energy regulator revenue risk and the liquefied natural gas procurement process costs.

How much of that NZ$4 million is going to LNG procurement? Well, look, there is a cost to, obviously, the procurement process. We can come back to you in writing with that specific answer to that question. That's very good. Yeah. We have a supplementary from Dr. David Wilson. Yes. Minister, thank you. Very complete. Talking about the gas transformation is really interesting in light of Mr. Willis's previous questions. We have options.

We've got electrifying of residential business, got geothermal, got biomass and so on. But a couple of the businesses I've been at just recently say, "Actually, it's almost impossible for us to electrify. We already have solar for parts of our business, but the other part we need gas for the right heat at the right time."

So do you think that there's an option here for us to tailor the gas, LNG, towards firming energy to support those businesses, mainly dairy? So what we've said as part of the negotiation between MV, NIFCo and the generator retailers is that part of the funding model should allow for a user pays element for those who wish to access gas, but they would have to pay for that as part of that agreement.

Ultimately, the budget also funds the gas transition loan scheme, or guarantee scheme, and that's designed to support businesses that can transition. But also, one of the requirements or the... So it's transition from gas to another form of energy, but it also enables companies who want to invest in kit which may reduce the amount of gas that they use.

So more modern and more efficient equipment as well. So I think the minimum reduction in gas use is 15%. So if they can demonstrate that, then that eligibility criteria is there for them. So the point of that is to support businesses and industries and to be able to look at what they can do to necessarily move from one to the other or reduce the amount that they consume.

But while I acknowledge there are many businesses out there which don't necessarily have options that are readily available to them today, and that is a significant reality for industrial base of New Zealand right now. And the way that the energy market currently works without LNG, the options for the gentailers is effectively either to scoop up what they can from lots of small businesses, which will, in the absence of methanex, if that was to happen, their options are to either scoop up what's left from small businesses and shut

those businesses, or for electricity prices to go to record highs, which will shut down a whole bunch of other businesses. That is economic carnage. That is demand destruction. That is the de-industrialization of New Zealand. And so the choices that we have in front of us are stark. They're challenging choices, but as a government, we have to act in the best interest of New Zealand.

Right. A supplementary, Dr. Vanisa Vini. And thank you, Minister. Mr. Willis mentioned around large scale solar and geothermal. Have you had any advice about the rates and the amount of uptake of solar and other sustainable things like geothermal? Yeah. So we are seeing a significant investment in the pipeline of renewable energy at the moment in New Zealand.

And I think that is a good thing. We're seeing a significant increase in what's being constructed and, as I said in my opening remarks, the government's fast track legislation is a part of our government's agenda to make it easier to build the generation that New Zealand needs. We've recently seen a new wind farm at the bottom of the South Island, I think it's the largest wind farm in New Zealand's history, just recently received fast track consent, which is significant. There's a pipeline of geothermal, wind, solar, battery

underway at the moment. Total forward pipeline, I understand, between '26 and '29 is about 11 terawatt hours of consented generation projects in the pipeline with more than 3.5 terawatt hours in construction right now. Fantastic. And so there is a significant pipeline currently underway, and that is growing as fast track continues to deliver more projects to the market.

And so we're seeing that flow through as part of the government's plan. And I think an important point, the correlation with LNG and the price of LNG is that the renewables will always be cheaper to run, and that means it will always be cheaper to build, and LNG ultimately supports that build-out because it means that when the wind's not blowing, the sun's not shining, there is that backup generation available and it gives people who are building renewable intermittent generation confidence that they can have the backup generation that they need, which gives them the confidence to build it.

A supplementary from Honorable Megan Woods. Thank you. Just to finish up on the LNG stuff from me. Minister, you talk about this being now a charge on the electricity companies, not a levy on consumers. So what are the specific mechanisms that you're going to put in place to ensure that the power companies don't pass this on to their consumers, be it residential or industrial?

Well, ultimately, managing the dry year risk is part and parcel of what a major electricity generator, retailer should be doing, is part of running their business. And what they have been doing over the last number of years, because the dry year risk has not been solved, is they have been passing that on to consumers with the price of electricity being around $30 to $50 a- A megawatt hour more expensive than the long-run marginal cost of production. And so they have the reality of not fixing this problem, is that the externalizing of that problem

to the households and businesses of New Zealand who have been paying the price of this problem not being fixed. So- So we have been paying the price of this problem not being fixed. And ultimately what we will see, and what we have seen, is through this decision already being made, is downward pressure on power prices.

This is part of what the gentailers should be doing as part of managing their business, and we expect them to do that. So there's no specific measures to- Well, down-... increase the... Sorry, I'll just finish the question. There's no specific measures to increase the powers for the EA in terms of enforceability because, a number of analysts are saying it's not the LNG decision, it's not the downward pressure, it's the lakes are full.

Mm. The sun's been shining, there's more generation coming on stream. There's a range of things that come in and shows the danger of correlation and causation muddling. Well- But I'm just clarifying, you don't have, in this year's work program, any specific measures to stop that passing? We are significantly increasing the powers in terms of the ability- But beyond that... for that, for the Electricity Authority to hold the major gentailers accountable with significant increased fines, something that you failed to do when you were the minister.

Okay. So just the last- Okay, I guess I'll finish up... the last wrap on this. All right. So, Minister, the government sits on two sides of the announcement, the June 9 package. So there's the proceeding with the procurement of the LNG terminal, then there's also the Winter Energy Reliability option, which requires large market participants to secure long-duration firm energy. Now, obviously in terms of the mixed ownership model companies, some very technical questions come into play here. So I'm just wondering what advice the minister

has received around being a 51% shareholder in those gentailers, and what steps are being taken or what advice that he's received to ensure that steps are taken to ensure they're not sitting on both sides of the commercial arrangement, and whether or not there'll be related party disclosures that will be required and the rights of the minority shareholders in those companies.

Well, I'm acting as the Minister of Energy in relation to the decisions that were announced on the- Absolutely... Tuesday the 9th- Yeah... of June. In terms of related party disclosures that the MOM companies have to make, obviously if that came into effect, they would have to give effect to those related party disclosure rules.

So that would mean that the 49% of shareholders would get to vote on whether or not this proceeds and the Crown would have to step aside? Well, there are rules in place when it comes to related party disclosures, and ultimately those rules are set as part of their trading obligations. As Minister of Energy though, I've been clear about the approach that we're taking.

We have entered into negotiations between MB and NIFCO and the gentailers. And also the point of the winter reliability obligations is very clearly about saying, as Minister of Energy, that the dry year risk sits with these companies to manage and to ensure that they address the risk that they have failed to do.

Have you had legal advice? In terms of? In terms of you being on both sides of this equation, in terms of the Crown- Well, as I'm here as-... being on both sides. Of the Crown being on both sides of the equation, of being a 51% shareholder and also making policy that is basically directing to a commercial decision.

So I've received advice around related party disclosures, or related party transactions. In terms of what I'm doing as the Minister of Energy though, is very clearly about putting the obligation where it should have always been, and setting policy in place which provides for that.

Will you release that advice? Well, look, I've received advice and it's subject to the Official Information Act. But the committee could request it. All right. Okay. Yeah. So Scott Willis, New Plymouth. Thank you. And, Minister, the price volatility of LNG wasn't modeled as part of MB's assessment of the suitability of an LNG import terminal.

So I wonder if you could explain why that wasn't the case and what you think the impact of the high volatility of LNG might have on households, given that there will be a pass-through some way through to households. Well, we've looked at the forward prices, and we've looked at, obviously, the impact of the situation in the Middle East and the implications of that.

And what we've seen and what the International Energy Agency says is that the forward prices show that the price comes down very quickly back within the range that we set when we agreed to this as in the initial cabinet decisions and in what's been released. And the International Energy Agency has pointed out that the amount of LNG that's coming online over the next three to four years well exceeds what has been disrupted through the war in the Middle East. And so, we have obviously looked at that particular issue.

In terms of volatility, in terms of prices, we rely on coal significantly. We're one of the few countries in the world which is transitioning from diesel to coal, but we rely on coal, and that also has its own price volatilities. It's also from one single market, one country, and so there's obviously risks there as well.

And Genesis as the importer- Of that coal has to manage those risks, and I think does manage those risks through normal commercial management of price volatility, which we would expect would be done in this case, too. And Minister, there's been numerous Official Information Act requests to MB on LNG from multiple organizations that have always been answered significantly overdue.

Are you concerned that this is giving the impression that MB's keeping inconvenient information out of the public arena until decisions are made, until we've already decided that this is where we're going? Well, my expectation is that MB operates within the requirements under the Official Information Act.

Even though they're overdue? No. Well, my expectation is that they give effect to the Official Information Act. I'm happy to have a look into that. If there's been some delays in responses, I'm happy to have a look into that. Good. That's good. I've got a new line. Yeah, new primary.

So was picking up on the de-industrialization arguments that the minister is putting up as the case for doing this. The concept modeling makes the case not only would LNG cost New Zealand more than it saves, but it also makes the case that the de-industrialization argument doesn't stack up. It makes the point that the clearest candidates to exit New Zealand and rather to switch to alternative fuels are gas-intensive, trade-exposed industries, but for most of these, gas is a minority fuel.

So it doesn't really see that as rescuing the case. Probably wood processing is one of the industries that we've seen hardest hit in terms of closures over the last couple of years. We've had Genesis Energy at select committee making the case for setting up the supply chain for biomass, whether that either be torrefied pellets or for just chip in terms of that. Has the minister seen that advice from concept on de-industrialization?

I'm not sure if I've seen that particular advice, but I have seen, obviously, there's a range of perspectives in terms of that issue. But what I have seen was what I experienced when I was the Minister of Energy in 2024 and what happened that year because the dry year risk wasn't managed. It was a significant closures of firms.

We saw prices go to significant heights. And actually, from my perspective, that's not something that we as a government want to see happen. So if the member's advocating for ignoring the problem and kicking the can down the road, which seems to be the approach of the Labor Party, then she's very much on the side of shutting down businesses, shutting down jobs, and putting our economy at risk.

It's not something that I'm prepared to do. Would note that when we made the announcement last week, a major energy users group said the government's package represented a significant step towards addressing long-standing concerns about New Zealand's energy security. That's something which we agree with. The BusinessNZ Energy Council Director of Advocacy, Catherine Beard, said LNG was a solution which can likely be implemented at pace to address immediate dry year concerns. So I think the voices of business are very much on the side

of making a decision and moving forward at pace in order to reduce the risk that they see their business is exposed to. Minister, your- I'll just ask a sup on that. But yeah, Minister, your officials have advice, and no doubt have been briefing you that this is going to cost New Zealand more than it saves and de-industrialization isn't an argument- I'll have to ask-... that can put in place of that. One of the things that I'd be interested whether the minister has asked for any advice about the impact of the decision to go ahead with LNG on the balance of payment.

We have clear choices. Far from saying that we should do nothing, I agree with the problem definition. I think there are alternatives that doesn't lock New Zealand into a highly volatile, high-cost pathway. If we look, for example, at using biomass in Huntly, that we have the opportunity to create jobs here in New Zealand rather than relying on imported fossil fuels from other economies. What we do know from the concept consulting modeling is the impact on the balance of payments over time, and has the minister asked for advice what that will

be, rather than creating homegrown energy solutions that create jobs and opportunities here in New Zealand, but instead wanting to subcontract that out to the rest of the world? I'll ask Nick to speak to the issue. So on the overall, how MB is taking into account these different models, we haven't looked at just one. That there are different modeling that find different things, and so we've looked across those.

And so our judgment around the gap of around three terawatt hours is based on a number of different sources, and a lot of that was in the business case late last year. To this issue on gas price and so on, there was some recent analysis that was commissioned by the Gas Industry Corporation that PwC did. In fact, that showed that under LNG, prices for gas would be lower because of gas going down so quickly, and the demand unable to transition fast enough.

So we are concerned about the ability for industry to transition in time and for the electricity system to transition in time. And so that's the basis of our, we do see the need for this, and that's why the- It's just- You know, that range of analysis. It's not just one. We've looked across to take into account what we think is a fair reflection.

So, thank you for that. In terms of the economic analysis, obviously, the Concepts modeling that you commissioned was quite comprehensive. Not all of it was made public. Will you now release all of that Concept modeling that was provided to MB?

We're happy to have a look at it then. I'm not sure of the exact reasons that we are sharing. Do you want to cover that? What? So we released all of the Concept modeling that we relied upon. So we commissioned Concept to do modeling to understand the price effects on the spot market of with or without LNG, and that was the modeling that we released in February this year.

Just this week, after some OIA requests, we released what is a draft slide pack. So that was never a finalized report. That was a slide pack that was the means of MBIE and Concept communicating kind of draft findings as they came through, and material has been redacted under the Official Information Act.

So this is the- And we're comfortable with the- Yeah. But the pack that you're referring to is the one that puts the cost to the New Zealand economy of $6.2 billion. You might be referring to the recent gas report in front of you. Which was not the... No, that report was not done for MBIE.

Right. That report was done of Concept's own bat. Yeah. MBIE has purchased a copy of it. Mm-hmm. And we are currently working through with Concept some of our concerns about their assumptions. So we're in the process. We think there were some overly optimistic assumptions about the way the gas market works.

So have you briefed the minister on that report? No, we have not. Has the minister had a copy? Has he asked for a copy? He's asked- He asked us yesterday for us to brief him this week, and so we will be doing so. When did you receive it? I received the updated report last week. Right.

All right. Scott Willis, you have a supplementary? With the rates. Or is that a new one? Yes, apparently some dodgy con-- dodgy assumptions as well, which I think that- Yeah. Supplementary. Have you... Were you looking at environmental emissions relating considerations in the procurement process? Is this part of the procurement process that you're undertaking?

In the procurement processes underway, and it's looking at the 3 PJ, or sorry, 12 PJ that's required over a period of three months in order to provide the three, the one and a half terawatt-hours of gas that's needed in order to be able to meet the dry year risk. We've also, obviously, there's a timeliness element to it. We need to make sure this is in place rapidly by winter 2028, which is pretty key in order to be able to support the New Zealand economy in the eventuation of a dry year in that year.

And so those are the key parts of the procurement process. So that's a no. Well, we're procuring a piece of infrastructure effectively. That's what we're procuring. Obviously, the use of the gas is a different part of the equation. But what we're currently going through is a procurement for the infrastructure. So if you're talking about construction emissions, I don't think that's a key criteria.

Right. Yeah. Would you like-- Well, I mean, are you suggesting things should be built out of wood? Great idea. Okay. Okay. So do you have a question? Oh. No, thank you. Yeah, no new primary for you? No new primary. I'm there. Okay. Supplementary. You have a new primary, is it? Supplementary. Supplementary, Dr. David Wilson. Yeah.

I'm interested in the timing issues that we're talking about some of these other alternative energy, such as geothermal, torrefied wood pellets, which is largely unproven, and the like. What are we dealing with here? Because obviously what we're talking about with LNG is filling that gap. So can you sort of elucidate on what some of those issues are with transition- Well, I think, I mean, there's a number-- No, I mean, it's a good question.

I mean, there's a number of issues that we're having to work through, and I think some people say, "Well, we could transition Genesis to biomass." And I think, I mean, Genesis is definitely doing some work in that space. But Genesis still only has three Rankines, which can produce 750 megawatts of electricity. Once those are firing at all full capacity, that doesn't... Which is effectively what the coal stockpile enables.

Mm. That only covers half of our half of the energy needs, and so therefore, you still need other kits to be able to deliver more energy into the market. And so that either means using existing capacity, which is gas-fired turbines, which we have at Huntly, or in other parts of the country. Just don't necessarily have the gas to be able to use them.

Or you have to construct new infrastructure, new kit to be able to burn it. And so, all of those solutions are potentially longer term solutions. But a key criteria is being able to do something quickly, because the reality is a dry year comes about, on average, every four years. We don't have the luxury of time.

We don't have the luxury to wait till 2037 to build a lake at the bottom of South Island. We don't have the luxury to wait for new technologies. But over time, those technologies will become more proven. They will become Um, cheaper or more cost-effective or more available. But in the meantime, we have to make a decision which ultimately protects our New Zealand economy.

Yeah. Mm-hmm. A new primary from Scott Ellis. Thank you, Madam Chair. I'd just like to switch to energy hardship space, because we've seen a 300,000 cap from this year's budget for the appropriation titled Assisting Households in Energy Hardship, and I'm assuming that's the support for energy education and communities bit. And we've seen in Budget '24 a cut of 1.5 million, the same program.

In Budget '24, there was also the cut for community-focused outreach programs to target the hard-to-reach households. Now, we know that there are over 600,000 households in energy hardship from data that was presented at the Hidden Energy Hardship Hui recently, and these are people who are really, really struggling.

Given energy hardship is something that has risen and is growing under this government, what impact is Budget 2026 cuts to energy hardship support, what impact will that have on affected households, on those 600,000 households already in energy hardship?

Well, there's a number of things that the government is doing to put downward pressure on power prices. The first one is around security of supply. Ultimately, that is critically important if we are going to have a market which provides affordable and abundant energy for New Zealanders. We need to make sure that we are doing that.

EECA obviously has the support for energy education and community, which provides funding to organizations that offer specialist energy advice and education to households. That plays a really important role. They have other advice and support to businesses in energy efficiency and energy use.

That plays an important role. The Electricity Authority has recently launched its new comparison in switching site, Billy, which gives consumers a simple way to check their plan and make sure they're getting the best deal, and I think that's a really important tool. Sarah can correct me if I'm wrong, but I think it's on average a couple of hundred dollars of savings to the consumer over the course of a year from actually making sure they're on the right plan.

And so that tool has been launched, and I encourage people to use it. Cabinet has also agreed to progress open electricity under Consumer Product Data Act to make it even easier for customers to compare electricity plans and make the best decisions for their households. So there's a number of things the government's doing in order to support customers and support consumers and provide a more competitive market. Of course, the Winter Energy Payment is available to those on either superannuitants or those on benefits.

And that plays a really important role to make sure that people who need that support the most, it's a targeted intervention to support those people who need that support the most, and that's in place from the 1st of May to the 1st of October to anyone receiving a main benefit pension or job seeker support.

So there's a range of different initiatives in place to provide support to those who need it the most. And supplementary. So while it may be a relatively small budget cut in this greater scheme of things, that the little bit that went to the support for energy education and communities really was one of those key bits of funding that went to get to those hard-to-reach households, and the minister may well assume that everyone is literate on computers and able to access all kinds of information.

But really, the type of households and the people we're dealing with are people who are really struggling, who may not have a computer in their house, who may have holes in the walls, who really do need whanau help and whanau navigation through these programs. And that's the type of program that has now been cut through funding. What does the minister think we can do to help those people who are really, really struggling, hard to reach, and- Well, I-... can't afford to heat their home to a healthy level and then become

clients of our health system? Yeah. So the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Authority does deliver programs in that space and will continue to provide programs in that space. But I put it to you, the retailers also have an obligation to make sure that they are doing their bit to provide information to their customers, and I encourage them to do their part to provide information and support to their customers.

They also have an obligation in this space, and I encourage them to pull their weight. Supplementary. Supplementary, yeah. So will the minister work with the retailers to seek, from the four retailers, at least $5 million per retailer annually to put into a fund to work with those hard-to-reach households?

Well, I agree. This is something that I think they're open to, and it would be helpful if we could have some bipartisan support on this. Well, I agree they should be doing more. Okay, so supplementary from Honorable Murphy. Thank you. One of the long-term programs that we've had to help households in energy hardships heat their homes is Warmer Kiwi Homes, and I note that there's no funding for the program in the budget beyond '27/'28. Is that correct?

Yeah. It was a funding cliff put in place by the previous government So, but this government hasn't put any additional funding in, in the three budgets that it's produced to extend that out? No. So it's a funding cliff put in place by the previous government. So but there's been three budgets, um, that this government has produced, and no additional funding has been put into Warmer Kiwi Homes, but and is not in this budget.

Just so my understanding is it's, uh, it was time-limited funding put in place. No. No, it needed to be topped up in subsequent budgets. It's choices governments make. But there actually was a cut to Warmer Kiwi Homes, to funding from Warmer Kiwi Homes in this year's budget as part of the energy funding reprioritization package. That's around, I think it was $8.5 million, um- It was a-... from memory uh, there was, um, s- an operational underspend from, from ECA.

It was not funding which was allocated to the actual delivery of the services. It was an operational funding, operational funding of ECA's, from ECA's baseline. So given there is no funding beyond 2027- Yeah... um, for this program, was any consideration given by you, Minister, uh, to put that underspend into the program so we could have more homes be insulated?

Uh, we've made, uh, choices around reprioritization, and obviously one of the initiatives that we have put funding towards has been the Solar on Schools program, which is going to deliver solar panels on schools for around 500 schools across New Zealand, which I think is a really positive, um, step forward. And we've made- Oh, don't disagree. I'm interested in people's homes- We've made-... and insulation of people's homes- And we've made-... with this question... and we've made choices to put that funding towards, uh, some of the underspends

towards some of those initiatives. That funding, my understanding, came from the reprioritization out of giddy. I'm talking about the 8.5 million that's been taken out of Warmer Kiwi Homes back into, into the general pot, and whether or not the minister gave any consideration, given he couldn't secure additional funding to continue this program beyond '27/28, whether he gave any consideration to putting that into further in- insulations of New Zealanders who are struggling with their power bills- Yeah, so the-... and can't heat their homes

... so this, this budget continues funding for that, uh, for the Warmer Kiwi Homes, uh, initiative. Obviously, there are decisions that will need to be made in future budgets around, uh, around the next steps. Um, but the underspend that the member is talking about was operational costs for, from ECA's baseline. Um, and uh, the savings in total from underspends around giddy, operational costs for Warmer Kiwi Homes, and discontinued- Yeah... crown funding for Ara Ake- Under source... come to, I think, around $57.35, uh, two

million over the forecast period. Given that there is no funding for Warmer Kiwi Homes beyond '27/28, and this is the first time in decades, this is a program that has been supported by governments on all sides of the house. So this is a first, that we've seen a government not put support into the ins- insulation scheme.

But it's funded in this budget. Um, it i- it is, it is funding that was already there, additional funding. What we have seen is over decades, that governments have made budget decisions to extend the Warmer Kiwi Homes out. It's exactly the same program that we inherited when we came into government and decided to keep funding in budgets that we delivered.

So what I'm interested in, has the minister received advice, um, around alternatives if the Warmer Kiwi Homes program is discontinued, about how support will be given to households that are struggling to heat their homes, to insulate their homes, and to pay their power bills?

Yeah, so the funding is in this budget for that program. Um, so- Through to '27/'28, but there is no funding beyond that. So- So, uh, what I want to know is a part of the advice- It's always been-... whether you've got alternatives. Yes. The fund- the funding is in that, uh, in, in the budget to continue the program. Obviously, future budgets will need to consider, uh, the fact that it, uh, you know, the next steps around, uh, this particular program and the options around, uh, and that advice will be forthcoming.

Um, the point is that the funding is there for, in this budget, for that program. The underspend is from operational sources. Okay. Uh, any more? Scott Willis. Thank you. Um, given that, uh, ECA's taken over Ara Ake's responsibilities, what, what organizational changes is ECA making to, to make sure that the innovation that was sustained by Ara Ake, New Zealand's Energy Innovation Center, will, will continue?

And I'm thinking there of the flex, hosting the Flex Forum- Mm-hmm... the, um, annual energy hardship hui, multi-trader relationships, those, those types of, that type of work that was happening with Ara Ake. What, what is going to happen now that it's moved to a different organization?

Yeah, so I can ask, um, CEO of ECA to speak to that. Okay. Good afternoon, scrutiny committee. Mm-hmm. So I'm Marcus Palener, CEO of ECA. We are in direct conversations with Ara Ake. I met with the CEO last week. Uh, basically they want to wrap things up under their own steam and then, uh, hand that over to us in a tidy way. So there's been no immediate decisions made because we're in conversations with Ara Ake.

Expect to have those conversations over the next few months. Thank you. Okay. Good. All good, Scott? Mm. Um- You can send some written questions later. We've got a lot of questions- Uh, no. One, one more little- Yep... yeah, so, so, um, given there's the Solar on Schools, which has come out of Ara Ake, um, program, it's a really positive step and why would, A, question one, why would we not go further? Why wouldn't we not put solar on every single school?

And secondly, is there any work underway, perhaps it's with the Electricity Authority, to think about the, the- That the actual cost of transmitting those electrons locally rather than the cost that we have or the value that's given to locally produced electrons. Is there any work underway to ensure that we can have peer-to-peer sharing of electricity, given that we will have schools in our communities that are not used at weekends and during the summer period when we will have the greatest solar production?

So in terms of the first part, so the first part of that question, 500 schools is what the funding that we have allocated enables, which I think is a great start. I think there's obviously future opportunity, which would be a question for future budgets. Future governments. In terms of the second part to your question, I will ask the CE of the Electricity Authority to speak to that.

Cool. Thanks. Good afternoon, everyone. In terms of your question about what I think you're talking about really is innovation and what some of the new ways or the new models might be. And as you may be aware, the Electricity Authority, so we're responsible for the rules of the system, and sometimes those rules need to be updated to keep pace with what's changing and new business models, new innovations. We really want to see consumers get the benefit of anything new that might ultimately help drive down total system

costs. So what we want to achieve is reliable electricity at lowest possible cost for consumers, and new business models can deliver that. And so one of the things we've done in the last few years, is establish what we call the Power Innovation Pathway, which is a way for some of those innovators to come in and engage with us. That helps inform the rules and whether we need to change the rules, in quite specific ways to accommodate things, that might be in the long term beneficial for consumers.

So, a shorter answer to your question, I suppose is yes, there is all sorts of work underway, and we can provide more information about specific programs of work. Maybe we sup on that. So 2019, there was the Network Transformation Roadmap, which was a fantastic document. Yes. Lovely words, but very little action in the EDBs. What powers does the EA have to compel the EDBs to be consumer-centric as they said they would back in 2019, consumer-centric in a way that will enable those peer-to-peer sharing of

electricity, the use of those local electrons to make the grid much more resilient but also more flexible, more dynamic? This is a topic we could talk about for quite some time, but I don't think that's the intention. So, first of all, your question is about what powers we have to compel the EDB.

So as a regulator, we are able to make rules for participants in the system that they have to comply with. And so electricity distribution businesses, lines companies, are participants in the system, so they are subject to the rules when we make them. That's the first point. I think the second thing that you'd be aware of is that, working closely with the Commerce Commission and EECA, we have written to electricity distribution businesses encouraging the sorts of things that we want to see.

And I'm aware also that the minister has previously written to electricity distribution businesses with the expectations. So there's a range of things that regulators and government can do to encourage the sort of behavior we want to see in the long run. One tiny- One final question. I am aware that you have some powers.

I do remember an advisory group, the Innovation Participation Advisory Group, some years ago, put a recommendation forward that boards of EDBs be compelled to demonstrate innovation and be sanctioned if they could not demonstrate innovation. Now, that was a report produced within the EA.

Mm-hmm. Did it go anywhere? Well, so I think you were a member of the advisory group at the time. And so it was an independent advisory group to the Electricity Authority. That advice has informed our work over the last few years, in terms of, I suppose, encouraging EDBs to innovate. Some of the things I've talked about are partly that.

There's also the Commerce Commission's regime, which I'm certainly not going to try and explain. That's not my remit, but that also has mechanisms to try and encourage innovation. What we want to see ultimately is that lower system cost, and so that can include things like non-network solutions that help provide...

Ultimately, they can avoid the cost of network build and those sorts of things I think we all want to see as ways to reduce the total system cost and benefit everyone. Yeah. So, members, we don't have enough time left for any more questions. So thank you so much, Minister. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you, everybody.
